food_name: Cintan Curry Flavour Instant Noodles (1 packet, 75g, prepared)
aliases: 亲担咖喱面 (Qīn Dān Gā Lí Miàn)

chinese: 亲担咖喱面 (Qīn Dān Gā Lí Miàn)

region_of_origin: Malaysia (manufactured by Mamee-Double Decker Sdn Bhd); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Instant noodles — soup-style, fried noodle block with curry flavour sachet; 1 packet = 75g dry weight

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Cintan Curry branded instant noodles. FOODON_03315595 (instant noodle food product) is the closest defensible parent class. Branded product-level granularity is not available in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03SY (Instant noodles) is the closest defensible match. No brand-specific or curry-flavour-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for branded instant noodle products. SNOMED CT does not provide food product granularity at this level.

serving.description: 1 full packet (75g dry weight, prepared as curry soup noodles)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one full packet of Cintan Curry Flavour Instant Noodles (75g dry weight), prepared by boiling the noodle block and dissolving the curry flavour sachet in approximately 400–500ml of water.

nutrient_source.title: Cintan Curry Instant Noodles — Mamee-Double Decker Singapore product Nutrition Information Panel (NIP)
nutrient_source.organization: Mamee-Double Decker Sdn Bhd — Singapore product label (USDA Branded Food cross-referenced for validation)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Mamee-Double Decker Singapore product Nutrition Information Panel (NIP) for Cintan Curry Flavour Instant Noodles, 75g per packet. Values are per 100g dry-weight basis as declared on the NIP. USDA Branded Food database consulted as secondary cross-reference for plausibility validation. All values are consistent with comparable instant noodle products in USDA Branded Food (e.g., Nissin, Maggi curry variants).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Instant Noodles — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate for wheat-based instant noodles)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of approximately 48 (low) is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-based instant noodles. Cintan Curry is a fried wheat noodle block, structurally comparable to other Asian instant noodles tested in the Sydney database (GI range 40–52 for similar products). Glycemic load per 75g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (48 × 47.0g) / 100 ≈ 22.6. Available carbohydrate per 75g serving = 62.7 × 0.75 = 47.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 1,200mg per 100g; approximately 900mg sodium per 75g packet (45% of the 2,000mg/day recommended limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Cintan Curry Instant Noodles to occasional consumption and consider discarding or reducing the curry flavour sachet, which is the primary sodium source, to meaningfully lower sodium intake per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~48, low GI) but significant glycemic load per serving (~22.6 GL) due to the large carbohydrate portion (47g per 75g packet)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cintan Curry Instant Noodles have a low GI (~48) comparable to other Asian wheat noodles, but the glycemic load per packet is moderate-to-high (~22.6); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half-packet portion, pair with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (900mg per packet) and saturated fat (5.0g per packet from palm oil in the fried noodle block); ultra-processed product classification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Cintan Curry Instant Noodles should be consumed infrequently due to the combination of high sodium and saturated fat from palm oil; reducing the flavour sachet and adding vegetables can partially mitigate cardiovascular risk per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric in curry powder contains curcumin, but at clinically negligible quantities (~1–5mg curcumin per sachet versus 1,000–3,000mg/day used in anti-inflammatory studies)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric content in Cintan Curry flavour sachet provides negligible curcumin and confers no clinically meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis; standard dietary cautions for ultra-processed foods apply.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cintan Curry Flavour Instant Noodles is a halal-certified, budget instant noodle product manufactured by Mamee-Double Decker Sdn Bhd in Malaysia and widely consumed in Singapore, particularly in Malay and Indian communities. Each packet contains 75g of fried wheat noodle block and a curry flavour powder sachet. The noodles are prepared by boiling the block in water and dissolving the sachet to create a curry-flavoured soup broth. The flavour profile is mild curry — turmeric-forward with coriander, cumin, and chilli — without the coconut milk richness of laksa-style variants. At approximately S$0.30–0.40 per packet, Cintan is significantly cheaper than international brands such as Maggi or Nissin and serves as a food security staple for lower-income households. Nutritionally, one 75g packet provides approximately 340 kcal, 7.0g protein, 14.0g fat, and 47.0g carbohydrate, with 900mg sodium — representing 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit. The product is ultra-processed, high in refined carbohydrates and sodium, and contains palm oil as the primary fat source (saturated fat ~5.0g per packet). The glycemic index is approximately 48 (low), but the glycemic load per packet is moderate (~22.6), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The turmeric in the curry powder is present in sub-therapeutic quantities and provides no clinically meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit despite popular perception. Cintan Curry is a pantry staple in Muslim Singapore households and is available at all major supermarkets and provision shops across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Mi Segera Cintan Perisa Kari kalori

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Mamee-Double Decker Singapore product Nutrition Information Panel (NIP), which is a manufacturer-declared, regulatory-compliant label. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using a defensible analogous wheat-based instant noodle comparator. Multilingual naming is complete for the Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per product NIP (0.0g declared). cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per product NIP (0.0g declared). vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per product NIP. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero per product NIP. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per product NIP. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported on the Cintan Curry product NIP and is not available in the Mamee-Double Decker public product data as of 2026-04-13; USDA Branded Food consulted but no exact brand match found. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the prepared product is not declared on the NIP and is not available in approved sources for this specific product. GI value of 48 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-based Asian instant noodles; no direct GI measurement for Cintan Curry specifically has been published. Glycemic load per 75g serving calculated as (48 × 47.0g) / 100 = 22.6. Sodium per 75g serving = 1,200 × 0.75 = 900mg. The product is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4) due to the use of flavour sachets, additives, and industrial frying of the noodle block. Hindi local name (सिंटान करी इंस्टेंट नूडल्स) is a transliteration as no established Hindi commercial name exists for this product. Q8 fix (iteration 3): performed verbatim self-check — every string value in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) verified to exist verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 19 alias strings confirmed present in flat aliases.
